1: "When a man takes a wife and marries her, if then she finds no favor in his eyes because he has found some indecency (ervat davar, H1320+H6961) in her, and he writes her a certificate of divorce (sefer keritut, H5612+H3748) and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house" — the divorce legislation: ervat davar ("nakedness of a thing" / "indecency of a matter") became the great point of rabbinic debate — the Shammai school interpreted it strictly (sexual immorality only) while the Hillel school interpreted it broadly (any displeasing thing). Sefer keritut (document of cutting-off): the written certificate provided legal protection — the divorced woman could remarry (v2) and was not subject to punishment as an adulteress. Matthew 19:7-8: Jesus is asked about this law by the Pharisees; he responds that Moses permitted divorce "because of your hardness of hearts, but from the beginning it was not so" — setting Gen 2:24 (one flesh) as the creation-ideal above Deut 24:1 as the concession-to-sin.
2: "And if she goes and becomes another man's wife" — the remarriage permission for the divorced woman. The certificate of divorce enables legal remarriage without the stigma of adultery. The divorced woman has full freedom to remarry: the certificate restores her to the legal status of an unmarried woman regarding future marriage.
3: "And the latter man hates her and writes her a certificate of divorce and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house, or if the latter man dies, who took her to be his wife" — the second divorce or death scenario creates the situation the law is actually addressing (v4): the original husband cannot take her back.
4: "Then her former husband, who sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after she has been defiled (hutamma'ah), for that is an abomination before the LORD. And you shall not bring sin upon the land that the LORD your God is giving you for an inheritance" — the re-marriage prohibition: the first husband cannot remarry his twice-divorced-or-widowed wife. The defilement language (tame, ritual impurity) applies to the changed covenant status: the first marriage has been legally severed and a second has intervened. The re-marriage would constitute a form of disguised divorce-and-remarriage cycle that the law prevents as prostitution-by-marriage-rotation. Jeremiah 3:1 uses this law as a metaphor for Israel's relationship with YHWH: "If a man divorces his wife and she goes and becomes another man's wife, will he return to her?"
5: "When a man is newly married, he shall not go out with the army or be liable for any other public duty. He shall be free at home one year to be happy with his wife whom he has taken" — the newlywed exemption: one year of home-freedom from military service. The legal protection of the marriage's foundation: the new covenant between husband and wife requires a full year of undistracted establishment. The law reflects a wisdom about human relationships: the early period of a marriage is formative, and disrupting it with military absence before the relationship is established is destructive. The one-year honeymoon protected by law is a striking humanitarian provision within ancient military culture.
6: "No one shall take a mill or an upper millstone in pledge, for that would be taking a life in pledge" — the millstone prohibition: the upper and lower millstones are the household's daily bread-production equipment. Taking them as loan collateral deprives the family of their basic livelihood. Ki nefesh hu chovel (for it is a life he is taking as pledge): the millstone represents life itself — the daily bread. The law creates a category of basic-livelihood items exempt from pledging. The principle underlies the broader collateral-law tradition (vv12-13, 17) that protects the vulnerable from having their survival-tools taken as security.
7: "If a man is found stealing one of his brothers of the people of Israel, and if he treats him as a slave or sells him, then that thief shall die. So you shall purge the evil from your midst" — the kidnapping law: man-stealing is a capital offense. The same crime is mentioned in the Decalogue's context (Exod 20:15; the commandment "you shall not steal" may originally have referred to man-stealing before being generalized). The kidnap-and-enslave crime attacks the fundamental freedom that YHWH granted Israel at the Exodus. The phrase vehi'thamer bo umekaro (treats him as a slave and sells him) describes the full commodification of a person, which is the extreme of covenant violation.
8: "Take care, in a case of leprous disease, to be very careful to do according to all that the Levitical priests shall direct you. As I commanded them, so you shall be careful to do" — the priestly authority over skin-disease diagnosis: the full legislation is in Leviticus 13-14. Deuteronomy's reference is a cross-pointer. The priesthood's diagnostic role in infectious-skin-disease cases maintained public health through ritual segregation and reintegration protocols. The Levitical priests are the public-health authority for the covenant community.
9: "Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the way as you came out of Egypt" — the Miriam-memory as warning: Miriam's leprous judgment (Num 12:1-15) is cited as the reason for taking skin-disease laws seriously. The reference serves as a precedent-reminder: even Moses's sister, even a prophetess, faced divine judgment for covenant violation while the disease-law was active. The memory is meant to induce carefulness, not shame: these laws are not arbitrary but reflect YHWH's holiness enacted in communal health protection.
10: "When you make your neighbor a loan of any sort, you shall not go into his house to collect his pledge" — the creditor's entry-prohibition: the lender may not enter the debtor's home to retrieve the pledge. The debtor brings out what he offers. The law protects the debtor's domestic privacy and dignity: the creditor has no right to search the home. The subtle honor-protection of the poor person's household space prevents the creditor from surveilling and controlling the debtor's living conditions.
11: "You shall stand outside, and the man to whom you make the loan shall bring the pledge out to you" — the procedure: the debtor chooses what to offer as pledge, within the constraints of the law (no millstone, v6). The debtor retains agency over what enters the pledge-relationship. The protocol normalizes the creditor-debtor relationship without dehumanizing the debtor: the loan is a service, not leverage for total control.
12: "And if he is a poor man, you shall not sleep in his pledge" — the overnight-cloak rule: if the poor person's only pledge is his garment (cloak), the creditor must return it before nightfall. The cloak serves as bedding for the poor person sleeping outside. The creditor's right to pledge is subordinated to the debtor's right to sleep in warmth. Amos 2:8: "they lay themselves down beside every altar on garments taken in pledge" — the prophetic condemnation echoes this law's violation.
13: "You shall restore to him the pledge as the sun sets, that he may sleep in his cloak and bless you. And it shall be righteousness for you before the LORD your God" — the righteousness-reward: returning the cloak at sunset is credited to the creditor as tzedaqah (H6666, righteousness). The charitable loan-management is covenant faithfulness that YHWH recognizes. The poor man's blessing (veberachecha) is not merely sentiment but a covenantal act: his gratitude is a form of covenant witness that YHWH honors.
14: "You shall not oppress a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether he is one of your brothers or one of the sojourners who are in your land within your towns" — the wage-laborer protection: lo tashaq (do not oppress/wrong). The prohibition extends to both Israelite and alien hired workers — the alien receives the same protection within Israel's borders. The economic vulnerability of the day-laborer (who depends on immediate payment for that day's survival) creates a specific obligation for the employer. James 5:4: "Behold, the wages of the laborers who mowed your fields, which you kept back by fraud, are crying out against you."
15: "You shall give him his wages on the same day, before the sun sets (for he is poor and counts on it), lest he cry out against you to the LORD, and you be guilty of sin" — same-day wage payment: the urgency is the worker's economic reality — ki ani hu ume'elav nefesh nasa (for he is poor and to it his soul is lifted up / he is counting on it for his life). The cry (qara) of the unpaid worker before YHWH is a claim YHWH hears: the unpaid wage is treated as a covenant violation with divine consequences for the employer. Leviticus 19:13 parallels the command.
16: "Fathers shall not be put to death because of their children, nor shall children be put to death because of their fathers. Each one shall be put to death for his own sin" — ish bekheto yumatu (each shall die for his own sin): individual moral and legal responsibility. This verse establishes one of the most important principles of justice: no corporate punishment for individual sin, no family-wide execution for one member's crime. Jeremiah 31:29-30 and Ezekiel 18 develop this principle prophetically against the proverb "the fathers have eaten sour grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge." The new covenant's individual-responsibility emphasis (Ezek 18:20: "the soul who sins shall die") is grounded in Deuteronomy 24:16. Romans 14:12: "each of us will give an account of himself to God."
17: "You shall not pervert the justice due to the sojourner or to the fatherless, or take a widow's garment in pledge" — the vulnerable-person justice mandate: three groups receive specific protection — the sojourner (alien), the fatherless, and the widow. These three form the recurring triad of the socially vulnerable in Israel's covenant ethics (Deut 10:18; 14:29; 16:11,14; 27:19; Isa 1:17; Jer 7:6; Zech 7:10). Their vulnerability is their legal protection: YHWH is their advocate, and perverting their justice is direct opposition to YHWH's own character (Deut 10:18: "he executes justice for the fatherless and the widow").
18: "You shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you from there; therefore I command you to do this" — the Egypt-memory as ethical foundation: the ground for protecting the vulnerable is Israel's own vulnerability-history. The Exodus experience creates permanent ethical obligation: those whom YHWH redeemed from vulnerability must not re-create vulnerability for others. The memory is not nostalgic but formative: Israel's identity as the redeemed determines their ethical obligations as the community of the redeemed.
19: "When you reap your harvest in your field and forget a sheaf in the field, you shall not go back to get it. It shall be for the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hands" — the forgotten-sheaf law: liqet (H3950, gleaning). The forgotten sheaf must be left for the vulnerable triad. The law institutionalizes generosity by prohibiting the retrieval of accidentally left-behind produce. The book of Ruth dramatizes this law: Ruth gleans in Boaz's field under this legal provision (Ruth 2:2-3,15-17), and Boaz exceeds the minimum by instructing his workers to leave extra (Ruth 2:16). The gleaning law is the legal infrastructure of the love-story between Ruth and Boaz.
20: "When you beat your olive trees, you shall not go over them again. It shall be for the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow" — the olive-harvest leftover: after the beating-harvest (knocking olives from branches), the remaining olives on the highest branches must be left. The three-vulnerability-triad receives equal protection across grain (v19), olive (v20), and grape (v21) harvests. The systematic inclusion of all three major agricultural products ensures the vulnerable have a legal claim across the full harvest cycle.
21: "When you gather the grapes of your vineyard, you shall not strip it afterward. It shall be for the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow" — the vineyard-gleaning law: after the main grape harvest, the remaining clusters must be left. The entirety of the agricultural economy is structured so that the vulnerable have legal access to a portion of every harvest. This is the institutionalized generosity of the covenant economy: not charity as an optional virtue but legal obligation built into harvest practice.
22: "You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt; therefore I command you to do this" — the Egypt-memory refrain concludes the gleaning laws as it concluded the wage law (v18). The repetition is structural: the entire social-welfare section of Deuteronomy 24 is framed by the slave-in-Egypt memory. Israel's identity as the liberated people requires them to structure their economy so that the vulnerable among them experience what Israel experienced from YHWH: provision in vulnerability, rescue from want. The gleaning laws are the economic embodiment of the Exodus story.
